speaker: interviewer
text: Steinweis And we're picking up where you were saying they were going from door to door, asking for gold and jewelry. And take it from there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then the people from the committee in Lublin, the representative from, from the Jewish population there, they took whoever gave something to stop that shooting. They went to that man. I saw him, only I forgot his name. He was a tall SS man dressed in black. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] In, in Poland, they used to be dressed-- the SS men-- black. I don't know why. And, and over there in Germany was a different color, a green color. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they went on to that SS man. And they gave him the silver thing. And he promised them he is not going to shoot anymore, any Jewish soldier. He didn't shoot. He stopped. And then, I was there maybe-- in that camp-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] maybe a week-- maybe not a week, maybe five days. And then they're calling us on, on the outside. And they start to release the Jewish soldiers in Lublin, Lublin, to the street. OK. They're calling me out. And they say, you, you can go. Oh, I was a Germany soldier. He opened the door there. You go any place you want. You are not here no more. Go. I went out. And then I went to the city. I came to the city. I didn't know what to do because-- and then I, I was stopping by a window. And I was looking in. And behind is a man, as Jewish fellow. And he said to me, you know, you're going with me. Because I'm not alone here in the street. We are lot of Jewish people. We taking off all Jewish soldiers. When we will not take off you, they'll shoot you here in the street. Then his name was Wachholter. He was a Jewish man, a family man. And he was in the Jewish committee. And he took me home to his home. And he got three or four children at home, grown ups. He gave me a bed. Before he gave-- I have to take a shower. He gave me underwear, everything. He was treating me very good. I was with him maybe two weeks. And then he said, now you'll go home. Then he bought a ticket for me. I got appointment from [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], from the prison of war, from the office. I got appointment to release me to go home, all paper. And I got this always in my pocket. And that man, that Wachholter, took me to the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], to the station. And he said, now you have a ticket. You'll will have a train in a half hour. I'll wait. I'll wait here till you go.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I shaked hands with him, everything. I thanked him for everything. And I came home to Sosnowiec. It took two days. Because the trains, they didn't go regular like it goes in, in peacetime, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came home, it was my father home, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] my mother home. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] a few-- two brothers-- escaped to Russia. One left a wife with a kid. The other one left a wife with four children. Then my brother-in-law left, too, his three. And my brother-in-law still lives. He is 96 years old. And he lives in the Bronx. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And I was with my father. And I was with my brother who died a year and a half. He was living here across the street. And the rest were maybe 10 children. I was the youngest. I was not married. It was maybe 10 children-- sister-in-laws, two sister. I got, two sisters and one brother, who were [INAUDIBLE] this time. Then from the beginning, it was not so good. It was very bad. Because my, my mother was very sick. She got cancer. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I start-- and they told me, you are the only man. What you have to see to it to, to take care of the children, too. Because the women, they could not help themselves. Say what, I can take care-- to hide them, to save them. I, I promise I'll do that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [CRYING]

speaker: interviewer
text: It's OK. [CRYING]

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's OK. So now you-- so what did you do next? What happened next?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm sorry.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's all right.

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't help myself, [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] because I, I mentioned the children. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then, OK, then first of all, I got to make a living. Then I, I took in a machine. And I was working to make some money, to make a living. And then whatever I can, I'll do for you, for the-- I mean, for their nephew and nieces. Only later on, you know, at home in this time, was the-- they made always a [INAUDIBLE], a committee, and joined with a [INAUDIBLE]. In Jewish, we say, [HEBREW SPEECH]. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And they, the, the Nazis, did that. And have-- they will tell them what they have to do-- they want to-- they say something to the Jewish people. The, the committee, they have to be the leaders and to tell the Jewish people. Anything what they will want, they have to, to obey the orders and to tell them. Then I was to have somebody there. And the, and the commander, who was my friend, he was a policeman, a Jewish policeman without [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ammunition or a gun or a rifle. They didn't, they didn't got this. And I promised him only one thing. He got to tell me when the German will come. He got to tell me before. Because in, in the [INAUDIBLE], they know that. It came out of there that the Jewish people got to do with this and this. Then he got to tell me. I will tell nobody. Only, I want to save the kids. He told me many times. When I knew it, they're coming night time-- they used to come night time. You see, this is nothing. They used to put out reflectors in the city-- in the-- not in the city, in the small town [INAUDIBLE] to round up the Jews on the one place. When I knew this before, I was hiding the children. My sister used to have a, a friend. She was not Jewish. She was a Catholic. Only, my, my sister used to be in business. She used to have like a candy store. And she used to come in there. And she was her friend. And I took four children from the sister. And I put it there to the goy. I let her know my one sister who got the child to hide someplace the child. She did so. Then the other four children, who their brother was in Russia to hide, I took them. I was hiding them in a place. Nobody knew it. And they came. They took other children. They took other people away. And I saved the children for a long time, maybe a year, year and a half. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] anything what I could do for them, I did it. I remember I took my wife-- my, not my wife-- I say, m my sister's children to that goy. And they were, you saw a picture here from cemetery? Over there is a wall. And over there is a stable.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I put them in in that stable. And I said-- the youngest was three years old, five years old, seven years old, nine years old-- four girls. And I told the oldest one, you have to watch them to not to cry, to stay here. Your uncle-- they call us a Jewish uncle is a [? fetter. ?] And this time I said, I'll come back. Don't worry. And I will-- and they called [HEBREW SPEECH]. And I'll watch you. I bought them some food, something to drink. And when it was over, the fire. They took away other people. I came. And I took them out.

speaker: interviewer
text: And, and once-- and this time, they took my sister, too. Then I figured when the sister, they'll send away the sister the four children. And where will go with the children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I got a few hundred mark my pocket. I went to a Jewish policeman. What I knew, he. he likes money. And his name was [? Lazer. ?] I said, [? Lazer, ?] somebody gave me 200 mark. I, I didn't want them to know that I have 200 mark, or more money. And I have to give it to you. Only, you have to let out my sister. Otherwise, you don't get it. He said, I don't know how I'll do it. Because they were in a building, locked up. In the building was a new building. And the toilets they built in the halls, not in the apartments. Then I said to him, the toilets are ready there. They are not open yet. Let them-- my sister in the toilet. You take the key. And give me the key. Or you will hold the key. He let in my sister there. They send away all, all the women there-- [? went ?] away with the men. Later on, I took back the kids from the stable there. And, and I got the sister back. And then somebody, a Jew, he went to the-- not to the police. Only, he went-- it was one in the Sosnowiec. His, his name was Maryn. And he was the leader from Sosnowiec till Krakow. He used to have a building for himself. And he got his own police, the Jewish police. But they were recommended from the, from the Nazis. And, and he-- later on, a Jew went to his office. And he told him that David gave to $200 [? Lazer. ?] And, and [? Lazer ?] let out my sister. And other people, Jewish people, they sent away. Night time, the Jewish police took me out from bed. He said, David, you have to go. We don't want to hurt you. For you, you'll be [? right, ?] you'll be lucky. It will nothing happen. And they took me to Sosnowiec from [INAUDIBLE], Sosnowiec. And I don't know-- around 11 o'clock, I got an appointment with Maryn. I went up to him. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was sitting down waiting, like you're sitting in a court. And later, comes in the judge. Here comes in the judge, Maryn. He sits down.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was sitting by your chairs there. He said to me, listen, David, you have to tell me the truth. Or you gave [? Lazer ?] the money or not? And what you did with you kids? And what you do with your sister? And what you do with the money? When you tell me the truth, nothing will happen to you. I said, is this a good Jewish world to allow the Jew or just talking? I told him this in Jewish. I shake hands. I tell you the truth and do you do nothing to me. I don't have to-- he, he was too big to shake hands with me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only he said, I told you, say. Otherwise, you going to Auschwitz, too. [? But ?] [? him ?] the Auschwitz, he puts me in among the, the, the, the other people that were supposed to be sent away. And I will go, too. I said, I will tell you the truth.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, first of all, I have a right to buy out my sister. I have a right to save my nieces. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Tell me the truth. I have a right or not? He said, yeah. You have a right to do that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Then I gave [? Lazer ?] the 200 mark. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the other guy who went, [INAUDIBLE]-- he what-- what he told you, it's not nice from him. Only, he told you already. You know already.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then what you will do now? You send me away to Auschwitz? He said, no. You go home. And your sister go home. And the policeman's not going to be [INAUDIBLE] of a policeman. Only, he's going to be in Sosnowiec a policeman. This was the punishment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Only, the policeman didn't do nothing to me. He helped me. Only what he did to me, he took the 200 mark. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this, this, this [? Fall ?] what I got at home. Only the end was that when they took me, everybody went there-- all nieces, all women, sister-in-laws, all went to the gas chamber. And this was-- still, I was home there. They attacked us night time with the lamp. Night time, they came-- 3 o'clock, 2 o'clock night time. And we were packed in, in the shul, in a big place where used to be horses there. And this was all people from the, from the town. And they made separate. Who goes to Auschwitz was on the right side. Who goes to camp was in the left side. Who, who stays home is right in the front. It was three-- one, two, three. Only I missed here. But I got a argument with a Polack. I'm a tailor. When I came home, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then I worked-- I did some work for the tailor. And then he didn't want to pay me, the Polack. I said, when you will not pay me, I can't work for you. I work because I need the money to make a living. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then he went to the police. And he said, I don't want to work. Only I was smarter than him. I went in the [INAUDIBLE], to the mine, coal mine. And I went in the office. They hired this time Jewish people who wanted to work on the coal mine.

speaker: interviewer
text: But in Poland, before the war, a Jew could not work there. In the war time, the Nazis hired Jewish people. I went in there. And I said, I want to work in the coal mine. He said to me-- the officer-- where you want to work, down there? Or you want to work in open field?

speaker: interviewee
text: I said I want to work in open field, not down there. I'm not used to it to go down-- first, here. And later on, you change me. I'll go down there, too. I registered. He gave me right away a paper that I'm hired. And I have to come to work in a week. He gave me time, a week. And here I was at the police. But the Polack went to the police. And they arrested me. And I came off the police. And the policeman, right away, his name was Schmidt, a tall fellow, SS man-- and he came to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, you're going to be shot right on the spot. And I say to him, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. Why you going to-- I'm going to be shot? He said, because you don't want to work. I said, all my life, I work. Why I have not to work now what I need the money to make a living? And I took out from my pocket. I said to him, Herr, may I something to show to you, a paper? He said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Took out the paper. And I was showing him the paper from the coal mine. I registered to work. And he looks over. He said, oh, yeah, this is much better than you work by tailor. This, I will do something for the, for the-- for Germany. We need the coal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And to him he said, you look for other tailor. And this time, I worked on the coal mine, too, in [INAUDIBLE]. This time, I worked till they send me away. [? Oh, in ?] 1943 there? Yeah. May.

speaker: interviewee
text: Before.

speaker: interviewer
text: May of '42.

speaker: interviewee
text: May-- [INAUDIBLE] in December, I went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Markstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Markstadt.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Tell me a little  bit about that. How'd you get to Markstadt? And tell me a little bit about Markstadt.

speaker: interviewee
text: How long I was in Markstadt?

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to-- how did that happen? They-- how'd they get you there? And then let's talk about that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, oh, oh. Yeah. You see, they, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they made, again, they took me-- no, they gave out the order. Every, Everybody got to be by a building where the, the, Jewish [INAUDIBLE] was there, the committee. And who, who will not be there, when we catch him, will be shot on the spot. Then I went there on that place. And this time from [INAUDIBLE], they took me on a [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] to Sosnowiec. They called this in Jewish, [HEBREW SPEECH]. Now we-- It was on the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]. We went there. And over there was a lot of people arrested already, Jewish people, to send it away. Then they send me away, because I was in good condition with the health. They needed me to work. Then they sent me away to Markstadt from that place.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Markstadt like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Markstadt, when I came there, I just-- over there was watching the Wehrmacht. Because the Wehrmacht is the regular German army. And when I was a year there, they liquidated the, the Wehrmacht. And what I went to Blechhammer. And before I'm going to Blechhammer. I want to mention something here, what-- not a fight-- what, what I got a struggle to stay alive in Markstadt. In Markstadt, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went on the [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH] where we worked-- worked a few thousand people there. They, they used to make there, like-- it, it was hills there like, like-- it looked like snow. And this was chemicals. They used-- they used to make there all kind of chemicals. I think ammunition, too. I didn't work ammunition there. Most I worked Markstadt by fortification, by carrying cement from the came a transport with, with, with the train-- cement, like, to go 200-- 100 yard, I will say, with the cement on my back, to unload by the building. Or, or by the fortification, I worked-- I was working with the shovel.

speaker: interviewer
text: But cement fortification, they made bunkers. You know what means bunkers? Because when the American came and they bombarded, they went in hiding there. Not for us-- it was only for them. And this kind of work what I did. No, I, I was lucky in Markstadt. I was staying once. And I was working. And I see a SS man is watching me. Where I'm going, he goes. And he was suspicious. I said, what do you want from me? What I did wrong? And I turning around once, when he came near. And I see with the same man, I was in the Polish army in one room, a year and a half. And I say to him in Polish, [POLISH SPEECH]. You are [INAUDIBLE]? He said, yes. He called me [? Davidko ?] in Polish, you know-- [? Davidko. ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, I can't talk to you now. 2 o'clock, I will talk to you over there. He was showing me a house. Come there, and I will talk. And he was a soldier who came back from Stalingrad injured from here all the way up till here. The whole flesh was ripped out here. And there were broken, a leg. And he was in the hospital maybe six months. And they send him to watch the Jewish people. And he came to me where I was working. And he recognized me. I recognized him, too. And he was-- when I was in the army with him, again, when I was in the magazine, I gave him a lot of clothing. He was like a sergeant already in the army. I, I was-- I have to go back to the army. I told you already this story. Because I forgot to say that I was in a school, not a student. Only, I was a tailor there. And when he came to me, I gave him the best already. And he was sleeping with me. He was like my best friend. He was my best friend in the, in the, in the room what-- he was became a sergeant. And I, I was a tailor.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Let's stop right there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.
